metadata.description: Weather-triggered advisory rules for avocado farming in Kenya, derived from agronomic literature

sources.title: BIF Avocado Production Manual - CDAAC Kenya
Avocado Growing in Kenya - Jürgen Griesbach, ICRAF
Persea Mite Management in Avocados - Samuel Khabi, KALRO

rules.name: Phytophthora Root Rot - High Risk
rules.risk: Phytophthora cinnamomi zoospores activate and spread in waterlogged soil at 20-32.5°C (optimum). Root damage begins after just 24 hours of waterlogging. The most serious avocado disease worldwide.
actions: Check and clear all drainage channels around trees immediately
Do NOT irrigate — soil is already saturated
Inspect root crown for dark discolouration or bark cracking
If phosphonate (fosetyl-Al) injection is scheduled, proceed — wet conditions aid uptake
Apply mefenoxam soil drench if not already applied this season
preventive_actions: Apply mefenoxam soil drench before onset of rainy season, repeat 16-20 weeks later
Plant on raised mounds/berms in heavy clay soils
Use resistant rootstocks: Duke 7 (for Fuerte), Thomas (for Hass)

rules.name: Phytophthora - Pre-Season Prevention
rules.risk: Zoospores become active and mobile at season onset. Prevention timing is critical.
actions: Apply first mefenoxam soil drench now — should coincide with new growth before rains
Ensure all drainage infrastructure is functional before rains begin
Apply Ridomil (Metalaxyl) granules to soil around susceptible trees

rules.name: Anthracnose - Active Infection Conditions
rules.risk: Colletotrichum gloeosporioides sporulates and infects at 23-29°C with near-100% RH. Infections remain dormant until fruit ripens, then cause post-harvest losses up to 60%.
actions: Apply copper-based fungicide (e.g., Cupravit at 50g/20L) if not sprayed in last 14 days
Apply azoxystrobin at pigeon-egg fruit size (max 2 sprays/season)
Apply cyprodinil-fludioxonil every 7-10 days while conditions remain favourable
Prune to improve canopy airflow and reduce microhumidity
Remove dead fruit mummies from canopy and ground

rules.name: Anthracnose - Spray Window (Dry Break)

crop_context.variety_note: Hass and Fuerte are highly susceptible

rules.risk: none — this is an opportunity window
actions: Good window to apply copper-based fungicide — spray must dry on leaf surface for 4-6 hours
Morning application is best — spray upward into canopy to cover all fruit-bearing branches
Spray will be ineffective if rain occurs within 4-6 hours

rules.name: Cercospora Spot - Infection Conditions

crop_context.fruit_size_note: Fruits susceptible from quarter to three-quarter of full size

rules.risk: Pseudocercospora purpurea conidia require water/heavy dew to germinate. Losses up to 60-69% possible. Wind and rain spread spores.
actions: Monitor for small irregular spots on fruits and leaves
Apply copper-based fungicides every 14 days during extended wet weather
Apply azoxystrobin at pigeon-egg fruit size (max 2 sprays/season)
Remove dead twigs and fallen fruits to reduce inoculum

rules.name: Avocado Scab - Infection Conditions

crop_context.region_note: Common in humid lowlands — Kenya Coast, Nyanza, Western provinces

rules.risk: Sphaceloma perseae sporulates in cool moist/warm humid conditions. Produces corky lesions on fruit surface. Spread by wind, rain, and insects.
actions: Apply copper-based fungicides from end of flowering to harvest
Apply azoxystrobin at pigeon-egg size, follow-up 28 days later (max 3/season)
Apply Bacillus subtilis as preventive biopesticide
Remove dead branches and fallen rotten fruits

rules.name: Stem End Rot - Wet Harvest Risk
rules.risk: Lasiodiplodia, Neofusicoccum, and Fusarium spores spread via wind and water splash. Warm humid conditions favour sporulation. Harvesting in wet weather significantly increases stem end rot incidence. Losses up to 30%.
actions: Avoid harvesting today if possible — wait for dry conditions
If harvest is unavoidable, apply post-harvest fungicide dip (fludioxonil + azoxystrobin) immediately
Pre-cool harvested fruit to 5°C within 6 hours
Minimize time between harvest and cold storage

rules.name: Bacterial Canker - Drought Stress Trigger
rules.risk: Xanthomonas campestris symptoms typically appear during drought years. Drought stress and boron deficiency promote disease. Wet/warm/humid conditions then spread it.
actions: Maintain adequate and even irrigation to prevent drought stress
Check boron levels and supplement if deficient
Inspect end-of-line trees (lowest water pressure) carefully
Apply copper-based bactericide if symptoms appear
Sterilise pruning tools between trees

rules.name: Verticillium Wilt - Optimal Conditions
rules.risk: Verticillium albo-atrum optimum at 21°C. Causes serious damage on younger seedlings. Conidia travel in air.
actions: Monitor young trees for sudden wilting on one side
Avoid planting in previously infected soils
Use crop rotation with non-host crops before replanting

rules.name: Fruit Fly - Year-Round Breeding Conditions
rules.risk: Bactrocera dorsalis breeds year-round at 18-27°C in tropical environments. Losses can reach 100% of unprotected fruit.
actions: Install methyl eugenol baited traps (20 per acre) for B. dorsalis monitoring
Apply spinosad or malathion-based bait sprays starting shortly after flowering
Spray early morning or late evening for best efficacy
Collect and destroy ALL fallen infested fruits — bag in black plastic for solar heat treatment, bury 50cm deep, or burn
Eradicate alternative host plants nearby (especially guavas)

rules.name: False Codling Moth - Breeding Conditions
rules.risk: Thaumatotibia leucotreta optimum at 25°C, RH 80%. Up to 800 eggs/female. 2-10 generations/year. Larvae bore into fruit.
actions: Install pheromone traps (4 per acre) from pre-bloom onwards
Apply methoxyfenozide at 8 and 4 weeks prior to harvest
Weekly orchard sanitation of fallen fruit (reduces infestation by 75%)
Apply CrleGV baculovirus spray in late evening (UV-sensitive)
Apply Bacillus thuringiensis at first sign of infestation

rules.name: FCM - Heavy Rain Suppression
rules.risk: none — this is a suppression event. Heavy rainfall destroys FCM larvae and pupae.
actions: FCM pressure is temporarily reduced after heavy rain
Delay next FCM spray application — natural suppression is active
Resume monitoring after 7-10 dry days

rules.name: FCM - Biopesticide Application Window
rules.risk: none — this is an opportunity window for biological control
actions: Good conditions for applying entomopathogenic fungi (Beauveria bassiana, Metarhizium anisopliae)
Apply in evening for best results — UV degrades biopesticides
Add sugar/molasses to CrleGV baculovirus sprays to enhance efficacy
High humidity enables 80%+ reduction in single application

rules.name: Thrips - Outbreak Conditions

crop_context.critical_period: Young fruit up to 2cm / 2-3 weeks after fruit set
crop_context.note: Older fruit with thicker skin is less susceptible

rules.risk: Hot dry spells during flowering/fruit set favour thrips population explosion. Damage causes scarring ('alligator skin') on young fruit, reducing market grade. Up to 80% fruit cull possible.
actions: Scout flowers and young fruit — shake branch over white paper, count thrips
If >5 thrips per flower cluster, apply spinosad-based product
Apply abamectin when fewer than 5 immature thrips per leaf/fruit (preventive)
Spray early morning when thrips are less active
If rain is expected within 3 days, wait — rain suppresses thrips naturally

rules.name: Persea Mite - Warm Dry Outbreak

crop_context.note: Egg-to-adult in ~15 days at 30°C vs ~40 days at 20°C

rules.risk: Warm dry conditions drive rapid Persea mite population surges. Drought-stressed trees have reduced tolerance. Severe infestations cause defoliation, exposing fruit to sunburn.
actions: Shorten scouting interval to every 5 days
Check undersides of leaves along midrib and veins for silvery webbing
Action threshold: 70-100 mites/leaf or 7.5-10% leaf surface damage
Release predatory mites (Neoseiulus californicus, Galendromus helveolus) at minimum 2,000/tree
Ensure adequate irrigation to maintain tree health and stress tolerance
Avoid broad-spectrum pesticides — they kill natural enemies

rules.name: Persea Mite - Cold/Rainy Suppression Window
rules.risk: none — this is a natural suppression window
actions: Mite populations are naturally suppressed — use this window for cultural controls
Prune and thin canopy to reduce mite habitat before dry season
Remove alternate host weeds (milkweed, sow thistle) near orchards
Sanitise tools and equipment to prevent spread
Prepare biological control inputs for dry season rebound

rules.name: Persea Mite - Rainy-to-Dry Transition Rebound Alert
rules.risk: End of rainy season is the highest-risk window for explosive mite population rebound. Suppressed populations bounce back rapidly as conditions dry.
actions: Begin scouting every 5-7 days immediately
Pre-position biological control agents (predatory mites)
Remove alternate host weeds which may harbour mites that reinvade
Check for previously wind-dispersed mites on trees downwind

rules.name: Persea Mite - Wind Dispersal Alert
rules.risk: Wind is the primary Persea mite dispersal mechanism via silk strands. Windy + dry conditions enable spread from infested to clean trees.
actions: After wind events, extend scouting to previously clean trees downwind
Inspect orchard perimeters and alternate host plants near windward borders
Enforce movement hygiene for workers, tools, and equipment

rules.name: Whitefly - Optimal Breeding Conditions
rules.risk: Honeydew from whiteflies promotes sooty mold, reducing fruit quality and marketability.
actions: Use bright yellow sticky traps to monitor movement
Apply neem-based pesticides (azadirachtin) at 7-14 day intervals
Use entomopathogenic fungi (Beauveria bassiana) in high humidity conditions
Rely on biological control (Encarsia, Eretmocerus parasitoids) as primary approach
Remove alternate weed hosts

rules.name: Shot-Hole Borer - Optimal Conditions
rules.risk: Euwallacea/Xylosandrus bore into healthy wood causing branch dieback. White 'sugar volcano' exudates are diagnostic.
actions: Survey for branch dieback and sugar volcano signs in warm humid conditions
Remove and burn/bury infested branches immediately
Apply Beauveria bassiana as biopesticide
Use quercivorol and α-copaene attractant traps for monitoring

rules.name: Lace Bug - Warm Weather Acceleration
rules.risk: Pseudacysta perseae life cycle completes in 21 days at 30°C. Higher temperatures accelerate multiplication.
actions: Do NOT apply pesticides during drought stress, wind, or freezing conditions
Apply insecticidal soap or narrow-range oil
Use Beauveria bassiana biopesticide
Maintain mulch and proper drainage to keep trees healthy

rules.name: Scale Insects - Development Conditions
rules.risk: Latania scale develops at 21-24°C, RH 79%. Infested fruit downgraded/rejected for export. Sooty mold from honeydew worse in humid conditions.
actions: Apply buprofezin or pyriproxyfen before first-generation crawlers move
Conserve natural enemies — avoid broad-spectrum pesticides
Control ant populations (ants protect scales from predators)
Maintain adequate tree spacing to prevent canopy contact

rules.name: Stink Bug - Optimal Development
rules.risk: Nezara viridula optimum at 25°C, RH 40%. Causes up to 25% fruit damage. Feeding distorts fruits and causes premature drop.
actions: Apply lambda-cyhalothrin early morning or late evening, not against wind
Use Crotalaria rattlepod as trap-border crops
Weed and prune to remove infested materials

rules.name: Broad Mite - Dusty Dry Conditions

crop_context.note: Fruits on shaded side especially affected; egg-to-adult 4.1 days at 25°C

rules.risk: Dusty dry conditions promote mite outbreaks. Toxic saliva causes twisted, hardened growth, aborted blooms, deformed fruits.
actions: Prune to allow light penetration — broad mites sensitive to heat/sunlight
Maintain cover crops to reduce dusty conditions
Apply abamectin at first signs
Apply early morning or late evening

rules.name: Nematode - Optimal Reproduction Temperature

crop_context.note: Heavy rainfall suppresses nematodes (fills soil air pores) but promotes Phytophthora simultaneously

rules.risk: Pratylenchus vulnus optimal reproduction at 26°C. Can suppress plant growth by 50%.
actions: Apply organic soil amendments (farmyard manure) to improve plant tolerance
Use clean planting materials only
Soil test before planting in new areas

rules.name: Heat Stress Alert
rules.risk: Above 33°C, avocado productivity and tree health decline. Above 35°C, fruit yield and size severely affected. Combined with low humidity increases sunburn risk.
actions: Ensure adequate irrigation to reduce heat stress
Shade young trees if possible
Monitor for fruit drop and desiccation symptoms
Do not prune — avoid exposing bark or fruit to direct sun

rules.name: Frost Alert
rules.risk: Avocado has very low frost resistance. Frost can kill trees. West Indian varieties have zero frost tolerance.
actions: Protect young trees immediately — cover with fabric or apply irrigation for thermal mass
Check variety-specific frost tolerance limits before deciding on intervention level
Inspect for frost damage after event — look for blackened leaves, bark splitting

rules.name: Fruit Drop - Sudden Temperature Change
rules.risk: Sudden chilling or sudden heat triggers physiological fruit drop at two critical periods (pea-size and egg-size).
actions: Alert farmer of likely fruit drop in coming days
Ensure irrigation is active to buffer temperature stress
Do not apply additional stressors (pruning, heavy fertilizer)

rules.name: Strong Wind - Structural Damage Risk
rules.risk: Strong winds break branches, cause flower and fruit drop, uproot young trees. Wind + rain on fruit-laden branches causes premature fruit drop and bruising.
actions: If harvesting, pause until wind drops below 20 km/h
Check tree stakes and supports on young trees after event
Collect any fallen fruit immediately — do not leave on ground (disease reservoir)
Inspect for broken branches — prune clean cuts and seal wounds

rules.name: Hot Dry Wind - Flower Desiccation
rules.risk: Hot dry winds desiccate flower buds and cause abrasion, reducing pollination success and fruit set.
actions: Alert farmer of likely poor fruit set
Ensure windbreaks are intact before flowering season
Increase irrigation to buffer evapotranspiration

rules.name: Wind - Disease Spread Risk
rules.risk: Wind spreads bacterial and fungal disease spores (Xanthomonas, Colletotrichum, Sphaceloma) across the orchard.
actions: Apply protective fungicides before predicted wind + rain events
Inspect orchard after wind storms for new disease entry points
Maintain windbreak integrity

rules.name: Sunburn Risk - High Radiation

crop_context.variety_note: Hass fruits specifically susceptible to sunburn

rules.risk: Sun-scorch damages stems, bark, fruit, and leaves. Most severe on western-facing side (afternoon sun). Combined with defoliation from mite damage, sunburn risk compounds.
actions: Whitewash exposed trunks and major limbs with 10% agricultural lime
Do not hard-prune during hot season — avoid exposing canopy interior
Ensure adequate irrigation to reduce heat stress

rules.name: Critical Post-Fruit-Set Irrigation

crop_context.note: The 2 months immediately after fruit set are the most critical water period

rules.risk: Moisture stress in the 2 months post-fruit-set directly causes reduced fruit size, poor quality, and fruit drop.
actions: Irrigate immediately if soil is dry — apply 40-50 m³/ha/day
Use drip irrigation only — never overhead
Apply no more than 50mm per irrigation event, but irrigate frequently
Monitor for leaf wilting and fruit shrinking as emergency indicators

rules.name: Kenya Dry Season Irrigation Alert

crop_context.note: January-March and August-September are Kenya's dry seasons

rules.risk: Without irrigation during these windows, trees suffer stress, poor fruit fill, and potential mortality for young trees.
actions: Activate drip irrigation — apply ≤50mm per event, frequently
For young trees (years 1-3), use water bottles during dry period
Increase soil moisture monitoring frequency
Maintain mulch to reduce evaporation

rules.name: Flowering Induction - Deliberate Dry Period

crop_context.note: Requires ~2 months of dry stress to induce flowering

rules.risk: none — this is a production management technique
actions: A 2-month dry period triggers flowering initiation (dormancy break)
Do not irrigate during this window for bearing-age trees
Monitor trees for dormancy signs — reduced flush, leaf curl
Resume irrigation after dormancy to trigger flowering and off-season fruit production

rules.name: Waterlogging Alert
rules.risk: Root damage begins after just 24 hours of waterlogging. Creates ideal conditions for Phytophthora cinnamomi. The single most dangerous condition for avocado.
actions: Check and clear all drainage channels immediately
Do NOT irrigate
Inspect root crowns for darkening or bark cracking
If waterlogging persists >24 hours, prepare Phytophthora fungicide application
Never allow standing water around tree bases

rules.name: Poor Pollination - Cold Flowering Conditions

crop_context.note: Bee activity severely reduced below 16°C daytime

rules.risk: Bees are much less active below 16°C. Significantly reduced pollination. Cold also delays pollen tube growth and disrupts A/B flower type synchrony.
actions: Alert farmer of likely poor fruit set this flowering cycle
Increase bee hive density near flowering trees
Apply foliar boron spray before/during flowering to aid remaining pollination
Postpone any pesticide applications that might further reduce bee activity

rules.name: Pollination - Rain During Flowering

crop_context.note: Occasional light showers do NOT harm flowers

rules.risk: Heavy/prolonged rain reduces bee activity and washes pollen from flowers. Can lead to flower shedding and reduced cropping. Light showers are NOT harmful.
actions: If heavy rain predicted (>10mm): alert farmer of likely poor fruit set
If light showers only: no action needed — flowers are not harmed
After heavy rain during flowering, monitor fruit set and consider thinning later if needed
Do not irrigate during or shortly after heavy rain at flowering

rules.name: Optimal Pollination Window
rules.risk: none — this is the optimal pollination window
actions: Excellent pollination conditions — ensure bee hives are in place (2-5 per hectare)
Ensure A and B flower type varieties are interplanted (1:9 ratio)
Do NOT spray any pesticides today — protect pollinators
This is the best window for maximum fruit set

rules.name: Floral Induction Temperature Trigger

crop_context.note: Required condition for transition from vegetative to floral buds

rules.risk: none — this is a positive phenological trigger
actions: Floral induction conditions are being met — flowering expected in coming weeks
Prepare for flowering management: arrange bee hives, prepare pheromone traps, schedule fungicide program
This is the time to apply foliar boron if levels are low (<40 ppm)

rules.name: Optimal Harvest Day
rules.risk: none — this is an opportunity window
actions: Good day to harvest — dry fruit stores better and has lower disease incidence
Harvest in the morning when fruit is cool
Use clippers, leave 5mm stem attached to reduce rot entry
Do not stack fruit more than 2 layers in crates
Move to shade immediately — pre-cool to 5-7°C within 6 hours

rules.name: Wet Harvest Warning

crop_context.variety_note: Fuerte especially susceptible to lenticel damage in wet conditions

rules.risk: Water droplets on fruit accelerate post-harvest disease. Lenticel damage more pronounced. Harvesting in wet weather increases vascular browning incidence.
actions: Delay harvest until dry conditions if possible
If harvest is unavoidable: apply post-harvest fungicide dip (prochloraz/Sportak) within 24 hours
Pre-cool immediately to 5-7°C
Fuerte: highest urgency to avoid wet harvest; Hass: lenticel damage hidden by purple skin

rules.name: Harvest Season Calendar - Kenya
rules.risk: Picking before maturity results in watery, tasteless fruit. Picking too late risks quality deterioration.
actions: Conduct maturity test: store a few fruits 7-10 days at room temperature (~27°C)
If fruit softens without shrivelling, crop is ready for commercial harvest
Match harvest timing to variety-specific calendar
Central/Eastern Kenya main season: March-October

rules.name: Post-Harvest Field Heat Alert
rules.risk: Internal fruit temperature reaching 26°C accelerates ripening, ethylene production, and fungal infection onset.
actions: Move harvested fruit to shade IMMEDIATELY — do not leave in sun
Pre-cool to 5-7°C within 6 hours of harvest using freeze-blast method
Cover transport vehicles with 80% shade-cloth (NOT plastic — creates warm humid environment)
Do NOT use banana leaves to cover fruit (produce ethylene)
Transfer to packhouse within 2 hours of picking in hot conditions

rules.name: Cold Chain Requirements
rules.risk: Breaking the cold chain leads to accelerated ripening and post-harvest disease.
actions: Pre-cool to 5-7°C within 6 hours of harvest
Store at 5°C, 85-95% RH
Hot-water fungicidal treatment: 50°C for 3-5 minutes (optional Carbendazim/Bavistin in dip)
Apply post-harvest fungicide dip (fludioxonil + azoxystrobin or prochloraz) within 24 hours
FCM quarantine treatment for EU export: 1.1°C for 14 days
Ripening room: 15-20°C (reduces rots vs higher temperatures)

rules.name: General Spray Window - Optimal Conditions
rules.risk: none — this is an opportunity window
actions: Good conditions for pesticide/fungicide application
Apply early morning or late evening when wind is calm
Observe wind direction before spraying — spray will drift in wind
Do NOT spray during windy or freezing conditions
Ensure protective clothing during application

rules.name: No-Spray During Flowering

crop_context.note: Kenya flowering: mainly Aug-Sep; off-season Mar-Apr

rules.risk: Broad-spectrum insecticides during flowering kill pollinators (bees), directly reducing fruit set and yield.
actions: Do NOT apply broad-spectrum insecticides during flowering
If pest control is unavoidable, use selective/bee-safe products only
Spray only in the evening after bees have retired
Maintain bee hives at 2-5 per hectare

rules.name: Prophylactic Fungicide Before Wet Season
rules.risk: Prophylactic spraying before wet periods is far more effective than reactive treatment after infection establishes.
actions: Apply copper-based fungicide now (pre-season coverage)
For anthracnose: apply from early fruit set, repeat every 14 days during wet weather
For cercospora: apply from end of flowering to harvest during wet weather
For Phytophthora: apply mefenoxam soil drench before rains
Ensure thorough coverage of all susceptible plant parts

rules.name: Nitrogen Application - Soil Moisture Required
rules.risk: Nitrogen on dry soil volatilises and is wasted. Nitrogen before heavy rain leaches away. Both waste fertilizer and can damage crops.
actions: Apply CAN/nitrogen fertilizer only when soil is moist
Avoid application immediately before forecast heavy rainfall (leaching risk)
If soil is dry, irrigate first then apply fertilizer
Split annual nitrogen dose into 2-3 applications timed to follow rains

rules.name: Foliar Zinc - Young Leaf Flush Required
rules.risk: Zinc foliar spray is only absorbed by young leaves — application at wrong time is completely ineffective.
actions: Apply zinc sulphate or zinc nitrate foliar spray ONLY during flush of young leaves
Old leaves do not absorb foliar zinc — timing is critical
Alternative: apply zinc as granules around trunk (less timing-dependent)

rules.name: Boron Application Before Flowering

crop_context.note: Especially important in high humidity/cool conditions where boron uptake is limited

rules.risk: Low boron during flowering impairs fruit set. Minimum 40 ppm boron required in floral organs.
actions: Monitor boron levels in leaf samples (take samples 2 months before flowering)
If boron < 40 ppm, apply foliar boron now
Apply by fertigation with irrigation water if available
Apply only when young leaves are present for foliar uptake

rules.name: Reduce Fertilizer in Dry Season
rules.risk: Over-application under low water availability favours pest development, causes nutrient imbalances, and increases salinity risk.
actions: Reduce fertilizer application rates during dry periods without irrigation
Avoid KCl (potassium chloride) to prevent soil salinization
Combine mineral with organic inputs

rules.name: Pruning - Dry Conditions Required

crop_context.note: Prune after harvest and before flowering; Oct-Dec in Central/Eastern Kenya

rules.risk: Pruning wounds are entry points for stem end rot and bacterial canker pathogens. Wet conditions increase infection risk at wound sites.
actions: Good conditions for pruning — dry weather allows wounds to heal
Sterilise pruning tools between trees (20% household bleach or 1.5% sodium hypochlorite)
Whitewash exposed trunks and branches to protect against sunburn
Maintain canopy height at 70% of row width, max 5.5-7m
Remove all dead and sucker branches

rules.name: Pruning for Disease Prevention - Humid Season

crop_context.note: Dense canopy traps moisture and creates disease-conducive microclimate

rules.risk: Dense unpruned canopy maintains high humidity at leaf and fruit surfaces, promoting fungal disease. Also reduces spray penetration.
actions: If canopy is dense and not recently pruned, schedule pruning for next dry window
An open canopy benefits from natural sun and air movement to prevent disease buildup
Post-rain canopy flush (after high-rainfall season) increases mite habitat — prune before dry season

rules.name: Planting Window - Rain Onset

crop_context.note: Seedlings should be ≥1m height, 6 months to 2 years old

rules.risk: none — this is the optimal planting window
actions: Optimal time to transplant seedlings — soil is deeply wetted
Water seedlings immediately after planting with 15-20 litres
Mulch around base to retain moisture
Whitewash stems with 10% agricultural lime to prevent sunburn
Stake young trees for wind protection

rules.name: Altitude-Variety Suitability - Low (90-800m)
rules.risk: Wrong variety at wrong altitude fails to thrive. West Indian race adapted to hot lowland conditions.
actions: Plant West Indian varieties only: Simmonds, Booth 7, Booth 8, Hardy, Waldin, Tambarina
Do NOT plant Hass or Fuerte at this altitude — they will underperform

rules.name: Altitude-Variety Suitability - Mid (800-2100m)
rules.risk: This is Kenya's primary commercial zone. Guatemalan race and hybrids perform best.
actions: Plant Hass, Nabal, or other Guatemalan varieties for commercial production
This altitude range covers most of Kenya's main avocado production areas

rules.name: Altitude-Variety Suitability - High (1500-2500m)
rules.risk: Mexican race and hybrids flourish at high altitude. Most cold-tolerant. Fuerte performs best here.
actions: Plant Mexican race varieties: Fuerte, Puebla, Bacon, Zutano, Ettinger
Zutano preferred for windy/cold-exposed highland sites
Fuerte preferred for export production at high altitude

derived_fields.description: Fields that must be computed from raw weather API data before rule evaluation

fields.name: cumulative_rain_72h_mm
fields.computation: Sum precipitation from hourly history (72h lookback)

fields.name: cumulative_rain_48h_mm
fields.computation: Sum precipitation from hourly history (48h lookback)

fields.name: cumulative_rain_24h_mm
fields.computation: Sum precipitation from hourly history (24h lookback)

fields.name: cumulative_rain_7d_mm
fields.computation: Sum precipitation from daily history (7d lookback)

fields.name: cumulative_rain_14d_mm
fields.computation: Sum precipitation from daily history (14d lookback)

fields.name: consecutive_dry_days
fields.computation: Count consecutive days with rain < 1mm from today backwards

fields.name: consecutive_dry_hours
fields.computation: Count consecutive hours with no precipitation

fields.name: hours_since_last_rain
fields.computation: Hours since last hourly observation with precipitation > 0

fields.name: temperature_change_24h_c
fields.computation: Absolute difference between current temp and temp 24h ago

fields.name: rain_trend_7d
fields.computation: Compare average rain in last 3 days vs previous 4 days: rising/falling/stable

fields.name: temperature_trend_7d
fields.computation: Compare average temp in last 3 days vs previous 4 days: rising/falling/stable

fields.name: is_early_morning_or_late_evening
fields.computation: True if hour is 5-8 AM or 5-7 PM local time

fields.name: is_daytime
fields.computation: True if hour is between sunrise and sunset

conflict_resolution.description: Rules for resolving contradictory advice when multiple rules fire simultaneously

rules.conflict: SPRAY rule fires but WIND rule says don't spray
rules.resolution: Wind rule wins — delay spray until wind calms. Never spray in wind > 15 km/h.

rules.conflict: SPRAY rule fires but FLOWERING rule says no insecticides
rules.resolution: Flowering rule wins for insecticides. Fungicides can still be applied if bee-safe.

rules.conflict: IRRIGATION rule fires but WATERLOGGING rule says don't irrigate
rules.resolution: Waterlogging rule wins. Never irrigate saturated soil.

rules.conflict: HARVEST opportunity fires but WET HARVEST warning also fires
rules.resolution: Wet harvest warning wins. Delay harvest.
Use a single integrated spray recommendation that covers all active diseases. Copper-based fungicides cover anthracnose + cercospora + scab.

rules.conflict: PERSEA MITE rule fires with broad-spectrum spray recommendation from another pest
rules.resolution: Avoid broad-spectrum pesticides — they kill mite natural enemies. Use targeted/biological control.

kenya_seasonal_calendar.description: Kenya bimodal rainfall calendar for rule timing
